text: The surgeon enters through the right upper pararectal area using an optical port, carefully splitting each layer of the abdominal wall without cutting the muscle fibers. Upon entering the peritoneal cavity, the port is turned parallel to the abdominal wall before being advanced further inside.
The surgeon introduces the second port, known as the anemone camera port, through the umbilicus. The telescope is then repositioned to the umbilical port for enhanced visualization.
The surgeon deflates the inflated stomach using a gastric calibration tube, preparing the organ for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon introduces the third port, a 5mm left upper pararectal port, which serves as the right-hand working port. The right upper pararectal port is utilized as the left-hand working port.

The surgeon begins devascularizing the greater curvature of the stomach opposite the incisura angularis using a ligature medullum vessel sealer. The greater omentum appears thin at or above the level of the incisura angularis, facilitating the devascularization process.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac and continues devascularization towards and beyond the pylorus. A bubble grasper retracts the stomach towards the left side through the left-hand working port, while a Maryland dissector is used through the right-hand working port to devascularize the greater curvature of the stomach.

The surgeon carefully dissects towards the pylorus, avoiding the pyloric antrum to prevent thermal injury to the stomach wall. The Ligasure Maryland device is used, with its insulated jaws providing protection while dissecting around the first part of the duodenum to prevent damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a bubble grasper through the epigastric port to retract the stomach anteriorly while dissecting towards the pylorus. The bulky greater omentum below the pyloric antrum is divided in layers, with dissection occurring between the gastroepiploic vascular arcade and the greater curvature.
The surgeon divides the greater omentum in layers, using the lower jaw of the Ligasure Maryland to push deep into the anterior layer before making the division.

The surgeon continues dissection five centimeters beyond the pylorus, below the first part of the duodenum. The first assistant uses a bubble grasper through the epigastric port to retract the stomach towards the left side. Once the dissection limit is defined, the remaining layers of the greater omentum are divided.

The surgeon retracts the pyloric antrum anteriorly using a bubble grasper through the epigastric port, while dividing the pancreatic-gastric attachments layer by layer. Care is taken not to hold the pyloric antrum or the first part of the duodenum with the tip of the Ligasure Maryland during dissection.

The surgeon divides the pancreaticoduodenal peritoneal reflection, carefully dissecting tissues layer by layer to reveal the course of the gastroduodenal artery. The pyloric antrum is retracted anteriorly to ensure safe dissection behind the first part of the duodenum without causing injury.

The surgeon carefully divides loose areolar tissue from the first part of the duodenum, ensuring not to injure surrounding structures. Branches of the gastro-duodenal artery are divided as encountered. A gauze soaked in diluted adrenaline is prepared to manage any bleeding. With the stomach retracted anteriorly, a thin peritoneal membrane of the lesser omentum is visible over the caudate lobe behind the stomach.
The surgeon divides the peritoneal membrane to create a window in the lesser omentum, focusing on the mobilization of the first part of the duodenum, which is a critical step in the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the gastro-duodenal artery and divides its branches to the first part of the duodenum. Dissection continues to separate the duodenum from surrounding structures, including adhesions between the transverse colon, gallbladder, liver, and duodenum. The stomach is retracted anteriorly to aid in the dissection below and behind the duodenum.

The gastro-duodenal artery is visible after opening the pancreatic duodenal peritoneal reflection, though it may be obscured by the pancreatic head. The surgeon continues duodenal mobilization by dissecting below and behind the pyloric antrum and the first part of the duodenum, as well as superior to the first part. The gastro-duodenal artery is seen beneath the jaws of the ligature Maryland dissector.

The surgeon carefully teases away the fat from the first part of the duodenum and divides the branches of the gastro-duodenal artery using a ligasure device through the left-hand working port, ensuring the duodenal wall remains uninjured.

The surgeon replaces the bowel grasper with a liver retractor through the epigastric port to facilitate dissection above the pyloric antrum and the first part of the duodenum. A blunt-tipped instrument is used to gently retract the liver, allowing for the division of the thin peritoneal membrane of the lesser omentum. The course of the right gastric artery is clearly visible, and care is taken to preserve the blood supply to the first part of the duodenum from the lesser omental vascular arcade.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully dissect above the first part of the duodenum, ensuring not to injure the gallbladder or common bile duct. The blood supply to the duodenum is preserved during this step, and carcharization is performed to aid in the complete mobilization of the duodenum.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the origin of the right gastric artery and continues dissection below and behind the first part of the duodenum. The pyloric antrum is retracted anteriorly using a bubble grasper through the left-hand working port, facilitating the dissection. Care is taken to stay by the side of the vertical limb of the L-shape near the upper margin of the first part of the duodenum to prevent injury to the common hepatic artery.

The surgeon uses the Ligasure Maryland to divide additional branches of the gastroduodenal artery supplying the first part of the duodenum, with the common hepatic artery visible below the instrument's jaws.

The surgeon dissects around the origin of the right gastric artery, clearly visible beside the upper margin of the first part of the duodenum. Pulsations of the common hepatic artery and the right gastric artery are observed. The origin of the right gastric artery is divided using the Ligasure Maryland without clips, taking care not to injure the gallbladder or common bile duct.

The surgeon divides an additional branch of the gastroduodenal artery and proceeds to dissect the lesser omental fat to complete the mobilization of the duodenum. The full course of the gastroduodenal artery is visible, and the right gastric artery is divided at its origin to ensure free mobility of the sleeve, pyloric antrum, and the first part of the duodenum.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the duodenum after dividing the right gastric artery. The remaining blood supply to the sleeve, pylorus, and first part of the duodenum is visibly maintained through the lesser omental vascular arcade, highlighting the importance of preserving these vessels to prevent anastomotic leaks.

The surgeon continues the resection upwards towards the esophageal hiatus, retracting the stomach to the right side using a bubble grasper through the left-hand working port. The liver is also retracted anteriorly along with the stomach using the same grasper. The greater omentum is pushed to the left side to identify the junction between the stomach and the greater omentum, allowing visibility of both sides of the greater omentum during division.

The surgeon uses a bubble grasper through the epigastric port to retract the stomach and liver, while another grasper through the left hand working port retracts the omentum towards the left side. As the dissection approaches the esophageal hiatus, care is taken to avoid thermal injury to the stomach wall. The pancreatic gastric attachments are released, and the blood supply to the upper pole of the spleen is identified and preserved, noting the proximity of these vessels to the stomach wall.

The surgeon divides the pancreaticogastric peritoneal reflection, carefully avoiding deep medial dissection to prevent injury to the left gastric artery, whose pulsations are visible. Using a Ligasure Maryland, the upper short gastric vessels are divided, while a bubble grasper through the epigastric port retracts the stomach and liver. Remaining pancreaticogastric attachments are divided, ensuring no injury to the gastroesophageal junction and the fundus of the stomach.

The surgeon continues dissection until the left crest of the diaphragm is identified, taking care not to injure the gastroesophageal junction, esophagus, or fundus of the stomach with the ligasure Maryland. After completing duodenal mobilization and devascularization, the gastric sleeve is fashioned starting four centimeters proximal to the pylorus, followed by duodenal division.

The surgeon uses an iDrive power gun with tri staplers, inserting the stapler through the right upper pararectal port. The stapler is fired, and clips are applied to any bleeding points. A gastric calibration tube is passed after the first stapler is fired, ensuring it does not narrow the gastric sleeve by staying away from the crow's foot.

The surgeon applies clips at the starting, junctions, and ending points of the staple line. Staplers are fired laterally to where the lesser omental vascular branches disappear on the stomach surface to perform a lax gastric sleeve. The anesthetist introduces the gastric calibration tube, which the surgeon maneuvers to the medial side of the first stapler firing.

The surgeon removes any loose clips at the end of staple firing and applies the second stapler lateral to where blood vessels disappear on the stomach surface. The stapler is fired through the left hand working port, ensuring symmetry by checking both sides of the stomach. Clips are applied at staple line junctions and bleeding points, avoiding excessive pressure to prevent interference with subsequent stapler firing. The sleeve is pushed medially using the stapler gun to ensure a straight staple line.

The surgeon fires the fourth stapler using Medtronic tri-staplers, applying clips at any bleeding points observed after stapler firing. The tissue compression occurs during the stapling process, and continuous stapler firing is employed.

The surgeon uses a Medtronic iDrive stapler gun to fire the fifth 60mm stapler, ensuring complete removal of the gastric fundus. The stapler is fired with a gap of 5mm to 1cm from the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon applies two clips on the sleeve side where the stapler has fired but not completely cut the tissue. Using a Ligasure Maryland, the surgeon divides between the two clips on the sleeve side and one clip on the specimen side, ensuring additional rows of staples remain on the sleeve side.

The surgeon places the gastric sleeve specimen over the right lobe of the liver and removes the gastric calibration tube. A blunt-tipped instrument is used to facilitate smooth division. The surgeon uses a 45 mm ply stapler to divide the first part of the duodenum, positioned two to six centimeters distal to the pylorus.

The surgeon uses a 45 mm ply stapler to divide the first part of the duodenum, positioned two to six centimeters from the pylorus, while ensuring the gastro-duodenal artery, common hepatic artery, and common bile duct are not injured. The stapler is lifted to retract the liver, maintaining blood supply to the duodenum without discoloration. The assistant gently retracts the liver. Surgicel is applied to cover the duodenal stump, with clips placed over any bleeding points on the staple line before covering with a single layer of Surgicel.

The surgeon demonstrates the mobility of the sleeve and the first part of the duodenum, folding the duodenum up to the level of the hiatus. Three-zero glyconate absorbable sutures are introduced into the peritoneal cavity. The greater omentum is pushed into the suprapubic compartment by holding its lower edge. The common channel length is measured from the ileocecal junction, with the surgeon standing on the patient's left side. Two bowel graspers are used for measurement: the right hand grasper through the epigastric port and the left hand grasper through the left pararectal port. Each measurement is five centimeters, and the common channel is measured to be three hundred centimeters by counting sixty times.

The surgeon uses bubble graspers with printed measurements to measure the small bowel, counting five centimeters at a time. The bowel is gently held over the antimesenteric border and slightly stretched before counting, ensuring not to overstretch. The measured bowel is pushed into the right iliac fossa, and the common channel length is initially measured to be three hundred fifty centimeters by counting seventy times.

The surgeon uses a bubble grasper introduced through the epigastric port to hold the small bowel and marks the 350 cm common channel location with a 3-0 glyconate suture on the mesenteric side. The transverse mesocolon is pulled anteriorly using the bubble grasper. The biliopancreatic limb length is measured starting at the duodenal flexure, with the surgeon returning to the primary position. The measured bowel is pushed into the right upper quadrant during measurement.

The surgeon ensures the orientation of the bowel is maintained to prevent twisting of the anastomosis. The measurement towards the ileocecal junction is completed, adjusting the biliopancreatic limb length to 390 centimeters and the common channel length to 300 centimeters. Three-zero glyconate absorbable sutures are used starting on the anatomical right edge of the first part of the duodenum, and a surgeon's knot is applied to approximate the duodenum.

An atraumatic grasper is used to retract the end of the suture towards the right upper quadrant, introduced through the epigastric port by the first assistant. The loop duodenal ileal anastomosis is performed in two continuous layers.

The surgeon applies sutures to the first part of the duodenum, moving towards the anatomical left edge. The outer layer is seromuscular, using absorbable sutures like 3-0 glyconate. A needle holder is used through the right hand working port, while a Maryland dissector is used through the left hand working port. Once the left side of the approximation is reached, a knot is applied. The first part of the duodenum is opened using a Ligasure device, with the option to remove or include the duodenal staple line in the inner layer.

The surgeon opens the ileum on the antimesenteric border using the Ligasure Maryland and applies suction to clear secretions. A 3-0 glyconate suture is used to create an inner continuous layer, passing the needle from outside to inside on the ileal wall and from inside to outside on the duodenal wall, with the knot applied externally. The suture is pulled snugly to ensure a watertight anastomosis, while an atraumatic grasper through the epigastric port applies traction on the suture end. If the duodenal staple line is intact, it is included in the running suture, avoiding blood vessel injury. The inner continuous layer is applied full thickness or extra mucosal, continuing from right to left until the starting point is reached.

The surgeon reaches the right end of the anastomosis and continues the anterior half of the inner continuous layer with full thickness extra mucosal sutures. The suture material is pulled snugly to approximate the cut edges of the duodenum and ileum, taking care to avoid injury to the blood supply. Extra mucosal bites are taken on both the duodenal and ileal walls, extending beyond any thermal injury at the cut edges. The duodenum and ileum are held gently to prevent serosal tears and vessel injury.

The surgeon completes the inner continuous layer of the anastomosis by applying a knot at the starting point on the left side of the patient, ensuring that full thickness bites do not include the posterior wall.

The surgeon completes the inner continuous layer of the anastomosis and removes the needle. The posterior half of the outer continuous seromuscular layer is then continued as the anterior half, moving from the left side of the patient to the right side. This additional outer layer is applied for extra security against anastomotic leaks.

The surgeon ensures minimal tension on the anastomosis by dividing the right gastric artery, facilitating a tension-free connection between the freely mobile sleeve and the first part of the duodenum. The sleeve and duodenum are maneuvered into the infracolic compartment for anastomosis, with suture material pulled snugly after each bite to approximate the edges.

The surgeon maintains tension on the previous suture while taking the next bite, carefully avoiding injury to the blood vessels supplying the first part of the duodenum. The anastomosis is rotated for better access as the final bites are taken. A knot is applied at the right end to complete the outer continuous layer.

The completed anastomosis is shown with two layers, eliminating the need for separate anchoring sutures. The surgeon covers the gastric sleeve with a single layer of surge cell, noting the absence of bleeding from the staple line but cautioning about potential post-operative bleeding due to blood pressure fluctuations or anticoagulant use.

The surgeon uses a sterile polythene bag to remove the rejected stomach specimen through the right upper para-rectal port, ensuring no spillage of contents. The right suprahepatic space is washed with saline after the specimen is secured in the bag. The port is then sealed from the inside using a Ligasure Maryland device.
